content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 6 – “P” for ProductPart 5 - New-product development strategy
Chapter 8 - New products: Developing and managing innovation
from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives
8.1 Explain how companies find and develop new-product ideas.
8.2 List and define the steps in the new-product development process and describe the main considerations in managing this process.
8.3 Describe the stages of the product life cycle and how marketing strategies change during the product life cycle.
8.4 Discuss socially responsible product decisions, and international product and services marketing.

Part 5  New-product development strategy

New-product development strategy (1 of 14)
A business can obtain new products in two ways:
through acquisition, i.e. by buying a whole company, a patent or a licence to produce someone else’s product
through the firm’s own new-product development.
New-product development refers to the development of original products, product improvements, product modifications and new brands though the company’s own research and development  (R&D) efforts.

Figure 8.1
The main steps in the new-product development process

New-product development strategy (3 of 14)
Idea generation
Idea generation is the systematic search for new-product ideas.
Truly innovative companies use extensive innovation networks to capture ideas and inspiration from every possible source.
Internal idea sources
Internal idea sources include:
R&D
company employees at all levels, e.g. executives, technical staff, manufacturing staff, salespeople.

New-product development strategy (4 of 14)
Idea generation (cont.)
External idea sources
External idea sources include:
distributors and suppliers
competitors
external new-product consultancies and design firms
customers.

New-product development strategy (5 of 14)
Idea generation (cont.)
Crowdsourcing
Crowdsourcing is the process of inviting broad communities of people – customers, employees, independent scientists and researchers, and even the public at large – into the new-product innovation process.

New-product development strategy (6 of 14)
Idea screening
Idea screening is the process of screening new-product ideas in order to identify good ideas and discard poor ones as soon as possible.
One new-product screening framework (R-W-W) is based around three questions – and the company should be able to answer ‘yes’ to all three before developing the new-product idea:
Is it real?
Can we win?
Is it worth doing?
Q: Elaborate on what each of these questions might mean in practice.

New-product development strategy (7 of 14)
Concept development and testing
It is important to distinguish between a product idea, a product concept and a product image:
A product idea is an idea for a possible product that the company can see itself offering to the market.
A product concept is a detailed version of the new-product idea, stated in meaningful consumer terms.
A product image is the way consumers perceive an actual or potential product.

New-product development strategy (8 of 14)
Concept development and testing (cont.)
Concept testing
Concept testing refers to testing new-product concepts with a group of target consumers to determine if the concepts have strong consumer appeal.
The concept can be presented symbolically (words, picture) or as a more concrete, physical representation.

Table 8.1
Questions for battery-powered electric car concept test
1.
Do you understand the concept of a battery-powered electric car?
2.
Do you believe the claims about the car’s performance?
3.
What are the main benefits of the battery-powered electric car compared with a conventional car?
4.
What are the advantages of the battery-powered electric car compared with a petrol–electric hybrid car?
5.
What improvements in the car’s features would you suggest?
6.
For what uses would you prefer a battery-powered electric car to a conventional car?
7.
What would be a reasonable price to charge for the car?
8.
Who would be involved in your decision to buy such a car? Who would drive it?
9.
Would you buy such a car (definitely, probably, probably not, definitely not)?
Q: Procter and Gamble (P&G) markets many haircare brands. Give examples of questions P&G might use to concept test a new haircare product.

New-product development strategy (10 of 14)
Marketing strategy development
Marketing strategy development refers to the process of designing an initial marketing strategy for a new product based on the product concept.
The marketing strategy statement consists of three parts:
The target market; the planned value proposition; and the sales market share and profit goals for the first few years.
The product’s planned price, distribution and marketing budget for the first year.
The planned long-run sales, profit goals and marketing-mix strategy.

New-product development strategy (11 of 14)
Business analysis
Business analysis involves a review of the sales, costs and profit projections for a new product to determine whether these factors satisfy the company’s objectives.
Business analysis includes such things as:
sales estimates
risk assessment
expected costs
estimated profits.
Q: What sources of information might P&G use to estimate sales, costs and profits for its new haircare product?

New-product development strategy (12 of 14)
Product development
Product development is a strategy for promoting company growth by offering modified or new products to current market segments; developing the product concept into a physical product to ensure that the product idea can be turned into a workable product.
The product-development step calls for a large jump in investment.

New-product development strategy (13 of 14)
Product development (cont.)
Test marketing is the stage of new-product development in which the product and the associated marketing program are introduced into more realistic market settings.
It lets the company test the product and its entire marketing program.
Q: Many companies seek ways to engage consumers in the product-testing phase, e.g. LEGO® invites kids to play with prototype toys prior to commercialisation. What are the advantages of involving consumers in testing and product development?

New-product development strategy (14 of 14)
Commercialisation
Commercialisation refers to introducing a new product into the market.
Here are some considerations for marketers when commercialising a product:
access to manufacturing facility
start-up marketing costs
when to launch
where to launch
market rollout.

New-product development strategy
A:
1. Can it be done, and would consumers want it?
2. Can we develop it more quickly and better than others?
3. Is it genuinely useful/needed? Would consumers pay for it, and would it be profitable for us?
Q: One new-product screening framework (R-W-W) is based around three questions
Is it real? 2. Can we win? 3. Is it worth doing?
Elaborate on what each of these questions might mean in practice.

New-product development strategy
Q: Procter and Gamble (P&G) markets many haircare brands. Give examples of questions P&G might use to concept test a new haircare product.
1.
Do you understand the concept of a shampoo-and-conditioner-in-one?
2.
Do you believe the claims about apply once and rinse out – a one-step process?
3.
What are the main benefits of a shampoo-and-conditioner-in-one compared with separate shampoo and conditioner?
4.
What are the advantages of the shampoo-and-conditioner-in-one compared with separate shampoo and conditioner?
5.
What improvements in the shampoo-and-conditioner-in-one would you suggest?
6.
For what uses would you prefer a shampoo-and-conditioner-in-one?
7.
What would be a reasonable price to pay for shampoo-and-conditioner in one compared with separate shampoo and conditioner? What do you pay now for your current separate shampoo and conditioner?
8.
Would you buy this product for yourself, for other household members? If you buy it for yourself would other household members use it anyway?
9.
Would you buy our shampoo-and-conditioner-in-one? (definitely, probably, probably not, definitely not)?

New-product development strategy
Q: What sources of information might P&G use to estimate sales, costs and profits for its new haircare product?
Their own internal information about similar products that they sell now.
Sales data: Industry reports such as IBIS. Neilsen sales data (sales by retailers for all brands in the category concerned).
Market research data about the proposed new product regarding likelihood that consumers would buy it and the price they would be willing to pay.
Product costing estimates from the manufacturing division.

New-product development strategy
A: Consumers provide an acid test of the new product concept. They can give feedback on whether or not they would buy and use the product, how, when, where and why they would use it.
They can also give specific feedback on the features and attributes they would want in the product and what features need to be added or can be removed.
They can also advise on the amount they would be willing to pay for particular features.
Q: Many companies seek ways to engage consumers in the product-testing phase, e.g. LEGO® invites kids to play with prototype toys prior to commercialisation. What are the advantages of involving consumers in testing and product development?

Coming up next

Managing new-product development,
Product life-cycle strategies and
Additional product and service considerations.
